title: Trie
description: A specialized tree for strings: sharing common prefixes across an unordered_map of children, enabling fast insert, search, and prefix queries.
objectives: Understand a trie as a tree specialized for storing strings by shared prefix
Implement insert and search on a trie
Understand why a trie beats a hash set for prefix-based lookups

sections.text: 1. Recap: A Specialized Tree for Strings

Binary Search Trees branched based on comparing whole values. A trie (pronounced "try," from re-TRIE-val) branches character by character, letting words that share a prefix share the same path through the tree.
A trie is a tree where each edge represents one character, and words sharing a common prefix share the same path from the root — with a marker on any node where a complete word ends.

Unlike TreeNode's fixed left/right, a trie node can have any number of children — one per possible next character — which is exactly why unordered_map fits here: it maps each character to the node representing "the path so far, plus this character."

sections.title: Why these three results differ
points: search("cat") is true — "cat" was fully inserted, and its final node ('t') is marked isEndOfWord
search("ca") is false — the path c→a exists (it's a prefix of both "cat" and "car"), but 'a' was never marked as the end of a complete word
startsWith("ca") is true — it only checks whether the PATH exists, not whether anything is marked as a complete word there
"cat" and "car" share the exact same c→a path in the trie — only the branches after 'a' (to 't' and to 'r') diverge

sections.text: 5. Why a Trie Beats a Hash Set for Prefix Queries

Recall unordered_set: checking whether an exact word exists is O(1) average — but checking whether ANY word starts with a given prefix would require scanning every stored word, O(n). A trie answers startsWith() in O(k), where k is just the prefix's length, completely independent of how many words are stored — exactly why tries power autocomplete and spell-check features.

sections.title: A phone keypad's predictive text, narrowing down as you type
sections.text: Typing 'c', then 'a' on a predictive keyboard narrows suggestions to only words starting with "ca" — the keyboard doesn't rescan every word in the dictionary on each keystroke; it's already sitting at the right branch, ready to suggest whatever comes next. That's a trie: each typed character moves you one step further down an already-organized path.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Confusing search() and startsWith() — search() requires isEndOfWord to be true at the final node; startsWith() only requires the path to exist.
Forgetting to mark isEndOfWord = true after inserting a word — without it, search() would incorrectly return false even for a fully inserted word.
Assuming a trie always uses less memory than a hash set — a trie can use MORE memory than storing raw strings, trading memory for prefix-query speed.
Forgetting that words sharing a prefix share nodes — inserting both "cat" and "car" doesn't create two separate 'c' and 'a' nodes; they reuse the same path.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a trie?
questions.answer: A tree specialized for storing strings, where each edge represents one character and words sharing a common prefix share the same path from the root, with a marker at nodes where a complete word ends.

questions.question: What is the difference between search() and startsWith() in a trie?
questions.answer: search() requires the final node reached to be marked isEndOfWord (a complete word was inserted); startsWith() only requires that the character path exists, regardless of whether it marks a complete word.

questions.question: Why is a trie better suited than a hash set for prefix queries?
questions.answer: A trie answers 'does any word start with this prefix' in time proportional only to the prefix's length, while a hash set would need to scan every stored word to answer the same question.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A trie is a tree specialized for strings: each edge represents one character, so words sharing a common prefix — like "cat" and "car" — share the same path through the tree, using an unordered_map<char, TrieNode*> at each node to branch by next character. isEndOfWord marks where a complete word actually ends, which is why search("ca") can be false even though startsWith("ca") is true — the path exists as a shared prefix, but nothing was inserted ending exactly there. This structure is why tries beat a hash set for prefix-based queries like autocomplete: startsWith() only costs time proportional to the prefix length, not the number of stored words.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Trie
sections.text: Trie = predictive text, one keystroke narrows the branch. Shared prefixes share the same path. isEndOfWord = a real word ends HERE, not just a prefix passing through.
